annotations.label: Laplace's equation

anchor.snippet: Laplace's equation is a second-order partial differential equation

annotations.note: Mathlib defines the Laplacian operator on inner product spaces, but Laplace's equation Δf = 0 itself is not packaged as a named predicate; harmonic functions encode it.

anchors.snippet: Laplace's equation is a second-order partial differential equation

annotations.label: Poisson's equation

anchor.snippet: This is called Poisson's equation

annotations.note: Poisson's equation Δf = g is not defined as such in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Harmonic functions

anchor.snippet: The twice continuously differentiable solutions of Laplace's equation are the harmonic functions

annotations.note: Defined as C^2 with Laplacian vanishing in a neighborhood, alongside `HarmonicOnNhd`.

annotations.label: Laplace's equation in rectangular coordinates

anchor.snippet: In rectangular coordinates

annotations.note: Mathlib gives the formula for the Laplacian as a sum of second partials in any orthonormal basis, which specialises to rectangular coordinates.

anchors.snippet: In rectangular coordinates

annotations.label: Laplace's equation in cylindrical coordinates

anchor.snippet: In cylindrical coordinates

annotations.note: No expression of the Laplacian in cylindrical coordinates is present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: In cylindrical coordinates

annotations.label: Laplace's equation in spherical coordinates

anchor.snippet: In spherical coordinates

annotations.note: No expression of the Laplacian in spherical coordinates is present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: In spherical coordinates

annotations.label: Laplace's equation in curvilinear coordinates

anchor.snippet: More generally, in arbitrary curvilinear coordinates

annotations.note: Curvilinear-coordinate / Laplace–Beltrami expressions are not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: More generally, in arbitrary curvilinear coordinates

annotations.label: Dirichlet problem

anchor.snippet: The Dirichlet problem for Laplace's equation consists of finding a solution

annotations.note: The Dirichlet problem is not defined as a named object in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Neumann boundary conditions

anchor.snippet: The Neumann boundary conditions for Laplace's equation specify not the function

annotations.note: Neumann boundary conditions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Principle of superposition

anchor.snippet: If any two functions are solutions to Laplace's equation

annotations.note: Closure of harmonic functions under sums and scalar multiplication is given by `HarmonicOnNhd.add`, `HarmonicAt.add`, and `HarmonicAt.const_smul`.

annotations.label: Uniqueness of Dirichlet solution

anchor.snippet: a harmonic function is uniquely determined by its Dirichlet boundary values

annotations.note: Uniqueness of the Dirichlet problem for the Laplacian is not stated in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: a harmonic function is uniquely determined by its Dirichlet boundary values

annotations.label: Neumann compatibility condition

anchor.snippet: the divergence theorem implies the compatibility condition

annotations.note: The integral compatibility condition for the Neumann problem is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: the divergence theorem implies the compatibility condition

annotations.label: Robin boundary condition

anchor.snippet: A third classical boundary condition is the Robin boundary condition

annotations.note: Robin boundary conditions are not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: A third classical boundary condition is the Robin boundary condition

annotations.label: Poisson kernel formula on the disk

anchor.snippet: the solution of the Dirichlet problem with continuous boundary data

annotations.note: Mathlib has the Poisson integral formula on disks via `poissonKernel` and `HarmonicContOnCl.circleAverage_poissonKernel_smul`.

anchors.snippet: the solution of the Dirichlet problem with continuous boundary data

annotations.label: Poisson integral in upper half-space

anchor.snippet: harmonic functions with suitable boundary data are represented by the Poisson integral

annotations.note: The half-space Poisson integral representation is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: harmonic functions with suitable boundary data are represented by the Poisson integral

annotations.label: Perron method

anchor.snippet: the Perron method , which constructs a candidate solution as the supremum of all subharmonic functions

annotations.note: Perron's method (and subharmonic functions in general) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Regular boundary point

anchor.snippet: A boundary point is regular if the solution of the Dirichlet problem converges to the prescribed boundary value

annotations.note: Regular boundary points for the Dirichlet problem are not formalized.

annotations.label: Wiener criterion

anchor.snippet: regularity of boundary points is characterized by the classical Wiener criterion

annotations.note: Wiener's criterion is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weakly harmonic function

anchor.snippet: is called weakly harmonic if

annotations.note: Weakly harmonic functions are not defined in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: is called weakly harmonic if

annotations.label: Weyl's lemma

anchor.snippet: By Weyl's lemma , every weakly harmonic function is in fact smooth

annotations.note: Weyl's lemma on the regularity of weakly harmonic functions is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: By Weyl's lemma , every weakly harmonic function is in fact smooth

annotations.label: Dirichlet's principle

anchor.snippet: This is known as Dirichlet's principle

annotations.note: Dirichlet's variational principle is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: This is known as Dirichlet's principle

annotations.label: Kelvin transform preserves harmonicity

anchor.snippet: its Kelvin transform

annotations.note: The Kelvin transform is not present in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: its Kelvin transform

annotations.label: Real and imaginary parts of analytic functions are harmonic

anchor.snippet: The real and imaginary parts of a complex analytic function both satisfy the Laplace equation

annotations.note: Both `AnalyticAt.harmonicAt_re` and `AnalyticAt.harmonicAt_im` state this directly.

anchors.snippet: The real and imaginary parts of a complex analytic function both satisfy the Laplace equation

annotations.label: Conjugate harmonic functions

anchor.snippet: The resulting pair of solutions of the Laplace equation are called conjugate harmonic functions

annotations.note: The existence of a harmonic conjugate (as the imaginary part of a holomorphic completion) is provided, but the term `conjugate harmonic` is not introduced as a named definition.

anchors.snippet: The resulting pair of solutions of the Laplace equation are called conjugate harmonic functions

annotations.label: Polar coordinate harmonic example

anchor.snippet: For example, if r and θ are polar coordinates

annotations.note: The polar-coordinate example r^n cos(nθ) as a harmonic function is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: For example, if r and θ are polar coordinates

annotations.label: Solutions have derivatives of all orders

anchor.snippet: any solution of the Laplace equation has derivatives of all orders

annotations.note: Harmonic functions on ℂ are shown to be real-analytic, which in particular gives derivatives of all orders.

annotations.label: Power series and Fourier series connection

anchor.snippet: There is an intimate connection between power series and Fourier series

annotations.note: The formal correspondence between harmonic power series and Fourier series is not stated in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: There is an intimate connection between power series and Fourier series

annotations.label: Stream function

anchor.snippet: the resulting function is called the stream function because it is constant along flow lines

annotations.note: Fluid stream functions are not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: the resulting function is called the stream function because it is constant along flow lines

annotations.label: Velocity potential

anchor.snippet: The harmonic function φ that is conjugate to ψ is called the velocity potential

annotations.note: Velocity potentials for fluid flow are not defined in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: The harmonic function φ that is conjugate to ψ is called the velocity potential

annotations.label: Analytic functions correspond to fluid flows

anchor.snippet: Thus every analytic function corresponds to a steady incompressible, irrotational, inviscid fluid flow

annotations.note: The analytic-function/fluid-flow correspondence is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: Thus every analytic function corresponds to a steady incompressible, irrotational, inviscid fluid flow

annotations.label: Electrostatic potential satisfies Poisson equation

anchor.snippet: the electric potential φ may be constructed to satisfy

annotations.note: Electrostatics is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: the electric potential φ may be constructed to satisfy

annotations.label: Fundamental solution of Laplace's equation

anchor.snippet: A fundamental solution of Laplace's equation satisfies

annotations.note: The fundamental solution of the Laplacian is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: A fundamental solution of Laplace's equation satisfies

annotations.label: Fundamental solution in three dimensions

anchor.snippet: on a sphere of radius r that is centered on the source point

annotations.note: The 3D Newton kernel -1/(4πr) as fundamental solution is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: on a sphere of radius r that is centered on the source point

annotations.label: Fundamental solution in two dimensions

anchor.snippet: A similar argument shows that in two dimensions

annotations.note: The 2D logarithmic fundamental solution is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: A similar argument shows that in two dimensions

annotations.label: Green's function

anchor.snippet: A Green's function is a fundamental solution that also satisfies a suitable condition on the boundary

annotations.note: Green's functions for the Laplacian are not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: A Green's function is a fundamental solution that also satisfies a suitable condition on the boundary

annotations.label: Green's identity

anchor.snippet: we may apply Green's identity

annotations.note: Green's identities (∫uΔv − vΔu) are not formalized.

annotations.label: Green's function for the sphere via reflection

anchor.snippet: the Green's function may be obtained by means of a reflection

annotations.note: Method of images for Green's function on the sphere is not formalized.

annotations.label: Poisson integral formula

anchor.snippet: A consequence of this expression for the Green's function is the Poisson integral formula

annotations.note: The 2D disk version of the Poisson integral formula is formalized via the Poisson kernel; higher-dimensional ball versions are not.

anchors.snippet: A consequence of this expression for the Green's function is the Poisson integral formula

annotations.label: Mean value property

anchor.snippet: the value of u at the center of the sphere is the mean value of its values on the sphere

annotations.note: The mean value property is given for ℂ via `HarmonicOnNhd.circleAverage_eq` / `HarmonicContOnCl.circleAverage_eq`; not yet stated in higher dimensions.

anchors.snippet: the value of u at the center of the sphere is the mean value of its values on the sphere

annotations.label: Maximum principle

anchor.snippet: a non-constant harmonic function cannot assume its maximum value at an interior point

annotations.note: Mathlib has the maximum modulus principle for holomorphic functions but not a standalone maximum principle for harmonic functions.

anchors.snippet: a non-constant harmonic function cannot assume its maximum value at an interior point

annotations.label: Spherical harmonic function

anchor.snippet: Here Y ℓ m is called a spherical harmonic function of degree ℓ and order m

annotations.note: Spherical harmonics Y_ℓ^m are not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: Here Y ℓ m is called a spherical harmonic function of degree ℓ and order m

annotations.label: General solution as solid harmonics expansion

anchor.snippet: The general solution to Laplace's equation in a ball centered at the origin is a linear combination of the spherical harmonic functions

annotations.note: Expansion of harmonic functions in solid spherical harmonics is not formalized.

anchors.snippet: The general solution to Laplace's equation in a ball centered at the origin is a linear combination of the spherical harmonic functions

anchor.snippet: Gauss's law for electricity (Maxwell's first equation) in differential form

annotations.note: Maxwell's equations are not formalized in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: Plugging this relation into Gauss's law, we obtain Poisson's equation for electricity

annotations.note: Electrostatics is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchor.snippet: Poisson's equation reduces to Laplace's equation for the electric potential

annotations.note: Source-free electrostatics is not formalized.

anchor.snippet: is specified on the boundary of a region

annotations.note: Uniqueness of boundary-value problems for electrostatics is not formalized.

anchor.snippet: We can thus define a magnetic scalar potential

annotations.note: Magnetostatics and magnetic scalar potentials are not formalized.

anchor.snippet: The gravitational field is conservative and can therefore be expressed as the negative gradient of the gravitational potential

annotations.note: Gravitational potentials are not formalized.

annotations.label: Kakutani's formula

anchor.snippet: This is known as Kakutani's formula

annotations.note: Brownian motion and Kakutani's representation of harmonic functions are not formalized.

annotations.label: Harmonic measure (probabilistic)

anchor.snippet: is called the harmonic measure

annotations.note: Harmonic measure via Brownian exit distribution is not formalized.

annotations.label: Harmonic measure (analytic)

anchor.snippet: called the harmonic measure

annotations.note: Harmonic measure for a domain is not defined in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: called the harmonic measure

annotations.label: Harmonic measure density is Poisson kernel

anchor.snippet: harmonic measure is absolutely continuous with respect to surface measure, and its density is the Poisson kernel

annotations.note: The identification of harmonic measure with the Poisson kernel is not formalized.

annotations.label: Persides' Schwarzschild solution

anchor.snippet: solved the Laplace equation in Schwarzschild spacetime on hypersurfaces of constant

annotations.note: Schwarzschild spacetime and PDEs on it are not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: solved the Laplace equation in Schwarzschild spacetime on hypersurfaces of constant
